text: Barium esophagram demonstrated contrast flow from the esophagus into the right upper lobe bronchus.
Endoscopy revealed the presence of the bronchosophageal fistula.
Close-up is seen on the right.
The bronchosophageal fistula was chosen to be approached via a right thoracoscopy due to the patient's lung problem.
CO2 and sufflation with partial ventilation of the right lung was used for exposure.
Dense adhesions were noted between the right upper lobe and the mediastinal pleura on the right side.
These were cleared using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection.
The lung was retracted posteriorly and inferiorly and adhesions to the anterior aspect of the mediastinal pleura were divided using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection.

Care was taken to identify and preserve the phrenic nerve and to avoid injury to anterior mediastinal structures, including the superior vena cava, as can be seen here on the right side of the screen, as well as the underlying trachea.
The right vagus nerve was also identified and preserved.
Anterior dissection was continued inferiorly until the azagus vein was reached.
This dissection was carried from anterior to posterior until the edge of the fistula was reached.

posterior dissection was begun by carefully clearing all adhesions from the right upper lobe to the azagus vein and the dissection was continued in a cephalad direction carefully separating the bronchoesophageal fistula which can be seen in the field here just cephalad to the azagus vein
Using blunt dissection with endopenas, the bronchoesophageal fistula was carefully separated free from all remaining attachments, allowing potential passage of a stapler beneath.
A clear plane between the fistula and the azagus vein connecting the posterior to anterior planes of dissection.
This can be demonstrated here with the suction tip through this plane.
All remaining adhesions and attachments from the right upper lobe to the anterior mediastinum were cleared as much as possible prior to division of the bronchoesophageal fistula.
A vascular clamp is passed through prior to passage of the stapler.
to ensure that the stapler would be easily passed.
Using a thick tissue load of an endogia stapler, the proximal portion of the fistula was divided.
Multiple firings were required prior to complete division of the bronchosophageal fistula.
Care was taken to ensure that division of the fistula
Incorporate as little of the pulmonary parenchyma as possible.

After the fistula is completely divided, the right pleural space is filled with warm saline, and on-table endoscopy is performed with air insufflation, demonstrating no leakage of air across the staple line.
At the conclusion of this, the esophageal staple line is covered by vascularized mediastinal pleura using interrupted 2-O ethobond sutures.
After all sutures are tied, a Jackson-Pratt drain is placed in proximity to the staple line and the pleural space is drained separately using a 28 French chest tube.
Completion endoscopy revealed adequate closure of the fistula.
